era_context: The Roman Empire is in the Crisis of the Third Century (235–284 CE), with Claudius Gothicus in the early-to-middle years of his reign (r. 268–270 CE). His military successes against Gothic invasions are beginning to stabilize the Balkan frontier. In China, the Three Kingdoms period (220–280 CE) is in its final phase, with the Jin state preparing for its conquest of Shu Han (263 CE). The Sassanid Empire under Shapur I (r. 240–272 CE) is at the height of its power.

events.title: Jin state military preparations for conquest of Shu Han
events.description: The Jin state, under the leadership of Sima Zhao and his son Sima Yan, is preparing for a major military campaign against the Shu Han kingdom. In 262 CE, the Jin state is mobilizing military resources and planning the conquest that will occur in 263 CE. The exact details of 262 CE's preparations are not individually documented, but the broader context of Jin military buildup is clear from Chinese sources.
key_figures: Sima Yan (future Emperor Wu of Jin)
events.certainty_note: The Jin state's military preparations are documented in Chinese sources, though the specific events of 262 CE are not individually detailed. The chronology is reliable within ±1–2 years.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220–280 CE)

events.title: Claudius Gothicus's reign; Balkan frontier stabilization
events.description: Emperor Claudius Gothicus (r. 268–270 CE) is in the middle of his reign, during which he achieves some military successes against Gothic and other barbarian incursions into the Roman Balkans. The year 262 CE falls within this period of relative military success, though the empire remains fragmented and economically stressed. The exact chronology of individual campaigns is uncertain.
events.certainty_note: Claudius Gothicus's reign is dated 268–270 CE, but the specific chronology of individual campaigns is not precisely documented.
cross_references: Related to broader barbarian invasions during Crisis of the Third Century

events.title: Sassanid-Roman frontier conflicts; Shapur I's campaigns
events.description: Shapur I of the Sassanid Empire continues aggressive campaigns against Roman-held territories in Mesopotamia and Syria. The exact nature and scale of 262 CE's conflicts are not well-documented, but the broader pattern of Sassanid expansion and Roman defensive struggles is clear.

sources.name: Shapur I's Res Gestae Divi Saporis (inscription)

events.certainty_note: Shapur's inscription does not provide year-by-year detail. The chronology of specific campaigns is reconstructed from multiple sources with significant uncertainty.
cross_references: Related to Roman Crisis of the Third Century

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The Jin state's military preparations and Claudius Gothicus's reign are not disputed, though the specific chronology of individual events within these broader contexts is uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 262 CE is sparse and heavily dependent on later compilations. Chinese sources provide more reliable information about the Jin state's preparations for the conquest of Shu Han, though the specific events of 262 CE are not individually detailed. Roman sources remain dependent on the unreliable Historia Augusta. Sassanid sources are limited to Shapur's inscription. The record is heavily skewed toward military and political history; economic, social, and cultural developments are almost entirely undocumented.

graph_edges.note: Jin military preparations in 262 CE led to the conquest of Shu Han in 263 CE
